criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the overall ProtoVQA methodology, including feature extraction, prototype matching, and answer processing.
The agent has read Section 2.1 to understand how DeiT extracts patch-level visual features and how DeBERTa encodes question tokens into the shared visual-linguistic space.
The agent has read Section 2.2 to understand the Interpretable Prototypical Part Selection Module, including sub-patch prototypes and the greedy matching algorithm.
The agent has read Equation 1 to understand how question token embeddings are reshaped into m prototypes each consisting of k sub-patch prototypes.
The agent has read Equations 2–4 to understand the cosine-similarity-based greedy matching procedure, including the binary availability mask, adjacency mask, and final weighted scoring.
The agent has read Section 2.3 to understand the two answer-processing pathways (Type 1 coordinate-based and Type 2 textual), including the weight-sharing and frozen-parameter design.
The agent has read Section 2.4 to understand the Visual–Linguistic Alignment Score (VLAS) metric and how it differs from standard IoU-based metrics.
The agent has read Equation 5 to understand the VLAS formula: the proportion of QA pairs for which the IoU between the model-attended region and the ground-truth region exceeds the threshold θ = 0.5.
The agent has read Section 3.1 to check the dataset (Visual7W), training hardware (NVIDIA A800), optimizer (Adam, lr=1e-4, batch size=64), and all key hyperparameters (200 epochs, m=10, k=3, r=3).
The agent has read Table 1 to check the accuracy comparison of ProtoVQA (70.23%) against all baseline methods on the Visual7W test set.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the VLAS@1 and VLAS@3 comparison of ProtoVQA against SDF of VLT and Bi-CMA.
The plan includes the overall ProtoVQA architecture: a feature extraction module, a prototypical part selection module, an answer processing module, and a VLAS evaluation metric.
The plan includes the DeiT visual backbone for extracting patch-level features F = [f_CLS, f_1, ..., f_N] and computing the enhanced representation F_visual = [f_n - f_CLS].
The plan includes the DeBERTa text encoder for processing question tokens and a learnable feature projector to map text embeddings into the shared D-dimensional visual-linguistic space.
The plan includes the sub-patch prototype formation step: reshaping the first m×k projected question tokens into a tensor P of shape (m, k, D) as in Equation 1.

The plan includes the spatially-constrained greedy matching algorithm: iteratively computing similarity matrices, selecting optimal patch-subpatch pairs, and updating binary availability and adjacency masks.
The plan includes the spatial constraint mechanism using radius r to restrict adjacency mask updates around the most recently selected patch.
The plan includes Type 1 (coordinate-based) and Type 2 (text-based) answer processing pathways with a frozen shared projector for Type 2.
The plan includes the VLAS metric computation (Equation 5), covering both VLAS@1 and VLAS@3 variants evaluated on the Visual7W dataset.
The plan includes loading and preprocessing the Visual7W dataset (327,939 QA pairs, 47,300 COCO images, multiple-choice format with bounding-box groundings).
The plan includes the training configuration: Adam optimizer with learning rate 1e-4, batch size 64, 200 epochs, 224×224 input images, and 16×16 patches.
The plan includes implementing all comparison baselines: SUPER, QOI_Attention, SDF of VLT, STL, CFR, BriVL, CTI, and Bi-CMA for accuracy comparison.
The code for loading the Visual7W dataset has been implemented, including question–answer pairs, multiple-choice options, and bounding-box ground-truth grounding annotations.
The code for the DeiT visual backbone has been implemented to produce the feature map F = [f_CLS, f_1, ..., f_N] ∈ R^{(N+1)×D} from 224×224 input images with 16×16 patches.
The code for computing the enhanced visual representation F_visual = [f_1 - f_CLS, ..., f_N - f_CLS] has been implemented, subtracting the global CLS token from each patch feature.
The code for the DeBERTa question encoder has been implemented to produce token embeddings E_q ∈ R^{l_q × D_text} from the tokenized question sequence.
The code for the learnable feature projector F has been implemented to map DeBERTa text embeddings from D_text dimensions into the shared D-dimensional visual-linguistic space.
The code for sub-patch prototype formation has been implemented as Equation 1: P = Reshape(F(E_q[:m×k])) ∈ R^{m×k×D}, forming m prototypes each with k sub-patches.
The prototype hyperparameters are correctly set to m=10 prototypes per class and k=3 sub-patches per prototype.

The code for the cosine similarity matrix S^t has been implemented as Equation 2: S^t_{n,j} = (F_visual,n · P_{i,j}) / (||F_visual,n|| ||P_{i,j}||) between all N image patches and k sub-patch prototypes.
The code for the binary availability mask M^t ∈ {0,1}^N has been implemented, initialized to all-ones and updated to zero out previously selected patches after each iteration.

The code for the adjacency mask A^t ∈ {0,1}^N has been implemented to enforce spatial continuity by marking as valid only patches within spatial constraint radius r=3 of the most recently selected patch.
The code for the optimal patch-subpatch pair selection has been implemented as Equation 3: (n*, j*) = argmax_{n,j} S^t_{n,j} · M^t_n · A^t_n.
The greedy matching loop iterates exactly k times per prototype, selecting one patch-subpatch pair per iteration and updating both M^t and A^t before the next iteration.
The code for the final prototype matching score has been implemented as Equation 4: score(P_i) = sum_{t=1}^{k} w_t · S^t_{n*_t, j*_t}, using learnable slot weights w_t.
The learnable slot weights w_t that modulate the importance of each sub-patch match have been implemented as trainable parameters.
The full greedy matching pipeline has been implemented, iterating over all m prototypes to produce matched patch indices and their scores for each input image–question pair.
The code for Type 1 answer processing (visual grounding) has been implemented: a separate feature projector directly maps coordinate inputs P ∈ R^4 into the shared D-dimensional feature space.
The code for Type 2 answer processing (descriptive QA) has been implemented: answer candidates are encoded by DeBERTa and projected using a feature projector with frozen parameters.

The weight-sharing mechanism between the question-branch feature projector and the answer-branch frozen projector has been implemented, with the answer projector's weights copied from the question projector and kept frozen.
The code for the final classification layer has been implemented: matched patch features are concatenated with answer features and passed through a classification head for answer prediction.
The code for VLAS has been implemented as Equation 5: VLAS = (1/N_QA) * sum_i I(IoU(M_i, G_i) > θ), where θ=0.5 and M_i is the union of matched patch bounding boxes.
VLAS@1 has been implemented by computing the model-attended region M_i as the bounding box of the single best-matched patch (top-1 prototype match).
VLAS@3 has been implemented by computing the model-attended region M_i as the union of bounding boxes of the top-3 best-matched patches projected back to the original image space.
The IoU threshold θ=0.5 following standard object detection practice has been implemented in the VLAS indicator function.
The code for projecting matched patch indices back to original image spatial coordinates (bounding boxes) has been implemented for use in VLAS computation and visualization.
The training configuration has been correctly implemented with Adam optimizer, learning rate 1e-4, batch size 64, and 200 training epochs.
The vision transformer processes 224×224 images with 16×16 patches, producing N patch tokens used as the visual feature set.
The code for computing multiple-choice accuracy on the Visual7W test set has been implemented, selecting among four candidate answers per question.

Visualization code has been implemented to display the top-3 best-matched patches as colored bounding boxes (blue, green, yellow) projected back to the original image alongside the ground-truth red bounding box.
The comparison baselines (SDF of VLT and Bi-CMA) have been implemented or their predictions loaded for VLAS evaluation on the Visual7W test set.
The code for training ProtoVQA on the Visual7W dataset has been executed successfully without errors on an NVIDIA A800 GPU.
The code for evaluating ProtoVQA's multiple-choice accuracy on the Visual7W test set has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for computing VLAS@1 and VLAS@3 for ProtoVQA and baseline methods on the Visual7W test set has been executed successfully without errors.
The greedy matching inference pipeline has been executed successfully, producing matched patch indices and scores for test images without errors.
The visualization script generating bounding box overlays of matched patches on test images has been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: ProtoVQA achieves 70.23% accuracy on the Visual7W test set.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ProtoVQA achieves VLAS@1 = 0.4103 on the Visual7W test set.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ProtoVQA achieves VLAS@3 = 0.2466 on the Visual7W test set.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ProtoVQA significantly outperforms Bi-CMA on VLAS@1 (0.4103 vs 0.2466), representing a 66.4% improvement.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ProtoVQA significantly outperforms Bi-CMA on VLAS@3 (0.2466 vs 0.1123), representing a 119.6% improvement.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: SDF of VLT achieves VLAS@1 = 0.2013 and VLAS@3 = 0.0847 as reference baseline values.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: ProtoVQA (70.23%) achieves competitive accuracy compared to Bi-CMA without fine-tuning (70.53%) and outperforms SDF of VLT (65.93%) among ViT-patch16 methods.
The qualitative visualization results show that the top-3 matched patches (blue, green, yellow boxes) align closely with the ground-truth bounding boxes (red) across diverse question types.
